question: Explain what ECCS is and how it operates.

nuscale_ch01_0189.title: BTP 6-2, Rev 3: Minimum Containment Pressure Model for PWR ECCS PerformanceAll (B.1 thru B.3)Various
nuscale_ch01_0189.summary: This section explains that the guidance in BTP 6-2, Rev 3 regarding minimum containment pressure models for PWR ECCS performance is not applicable to the NuScale design, as a postulated LOCA in NuScale does not result in core uncovery. Therefore, the regulatory criteria addressed in BTP 6-2 do not apply to the NuScale reactor.

nuscale_ch01_0188.title: BTP 6-1, (March 2007): pH for Emergency Coolant Water for Pressurized Water ReactorsB.3 Hydrogen Generation from Aluminum Corrosion
nuscale_ch01_0188.summary: The section reviews NuScale's conformance with regulatory criteria regarding pH control and hydrogen generation from aluminum corrosion in emergency coolant water. While NuScale's design lacks certain systems referenced in the criteria (such as a containment spray system or sump), the intent of the requirements is still met due to the collection and recirculation of ECCS water within the containment vessel. Other criteria related to minimum containment pressure and bypass leakage are deemed not applicable to the NuScale design.

nuscale_ch01_0028.title: Instrumentation, Controls, and Electrical Systems
nuscale_ch01_0028.summary: The Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) in the NuScale design provides passive decay heat removal during a loss-of-coolant accident using independent vent and recirculation valves, operating without active power. The Control Room Habitability System (CRHS) passively supplies clean, compressed air to protect operators during emergencies, while fission product removal relies on the containment vessel and isolation system, utilizing passive processes for safety.

nuscale_ch01_0063.title: Emergency Core Cooling System Valve Design Certification Application Demonstration Test
nuscale_ch01_0063.summary: This section describes the demonstration test program for the Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) valves in the NuScale reactor design. The test program aimed to verify the functional performance of the ECCS valves, including their operation at reactor pressures and temperatures and in borated coolant, as well as the effectiveness of unique design features like the inadvertent actuation block (IAB). Testing was conducted at a specialized facility using a test article representative of the reactor recirculation valve (RRV) to ensure the valves meet safety and operational requirements.

nuscale_ch01_0024.title: Emergency Core Cooling System
nuscale_ch01_0024.summary: The Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) in the NuScale design is a fully passive system that removes decay heat during a loss-of-coolant accident (LOCA) using three independent reactor vent valves and two recirculation valves, all of which are closed during normal operation. During an emergency, the system vents steam into containment for condensation and recirculates water back to the core, with heat ultimately transferred passively to the reactor pool.

nuscale_ch01_0017.title: Facility Description
nuscale_ch01_0017.summary: The Facility Description section outlines the NuScale plant’s core cooling and power generation process. It describes how the reactor core heats coolant, which circulates naturally to transfer heat to steam generators, producing superheated steam that drives a turbine and generates electricity. The section also notes that site-specific details exist for cooling systems and equipment layout.

edges.description: Section A references the operation of ECCS and its interaction with containment water chemistry requirements, while Section B details specific pH and hydrogen generation considerations during ECCS operation, both connected by the regulatory evaluation of ECCS water chemistry in the NuScale design.
Section B provides a general description of the ECCS and its operation, which is referenced in Section A's discussion of regulatory conformance and specific design features.

Section B provides specific test results and operational details of the ECCS valves, which serve as concrete evidence supporting the general regulatory conformance claims about ECCS operation discussed in Section A.

Section B describes the design and operation of the ECCS, while Section A references regulatory criteria and acceptance conditions (including water collection during ECCS operation) that the NuScale ECCS must address, thus Section B's information is used to demonstrate conformance with Section A's regulatory requirements.

Section B describes the design and operation of the ECCS, while Section A references regulatory criteria (BTP 6-1) that partially apply to the NuScale ECCS operation, specifically regarding water chemistry and system components.

Section A discusses the regulatory criteria and chemical requirements for ECCS water during operation, while Section B provides test results confirming the operability of ECCS valves in the NuScale design, thus Section B supports the operational aspect referenced in Section A.

Section A references regulatory criteria for ECCS water chemistry and notes how the NuScale design conforms or differs, while Section B describes the design and operation of the ECCS in the NuScale plant, thus Section A's discussion of regulatory conformance is directly connected to the ECCS operational details provided in Section B.

Section A describes the function and configuration of the ECCS, while Section B provides test results confirming the operability of ECCS valves as described in Section A, thus supporting the operational claims made in Section A.
Section A and Section B are identical excerpts describing the components and operation of the ECCS, providing the same information about its function and configuration.

Section A describes the testing and design verification of ECCS valves, while Section B provides a general overview of the ECCS's function and components, with Section A specifying technical details that support and elaborate on the general description in Section B.

Section B discusses regulatory criteria for emergency coolant water chemistry during ECCS operation, while Section A provides a general facility and system description without directly addressing ECCS, thus Section B partially references and interprets requirements relevant to the systems described in Section A.

Section B explains the function and operation of the Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS), which is the direct answer to the analysis question, while Section A provides general facility and process context but does not address ECCS specifically; thus, Section B specifies the relevant operational details that Section A does not cover.
